When moving to a local project, create a `stacktape.ts` file (recommended) or `stacktape.yaml` in your project, then run [`stacktape deploy`](/cli/deploy) to deploy your stack, or set up [GitOps](/ci-cd-and-gitops/gitops-with-console) for automatic deployments on push. Use [`stacktape init`](/cli/init) when you're starting a new local project.

## Troubleshooting

### Autocompletion not appearing

Trigger autocompletion with `Ctrl+Space` on Windows/Linux or `Cmd+Space` on Mac.

### CloudFormation preview shows approximate values

The CloudFormation template view is a static compilation. It is not 100% accurate because some information is known only during deployment. This is expected behavior — the actual deployed template resolves all values at deploy time.

### Config linked to a stage is read-only

Templates marked as created in the Console for a new stage are read-only in the config editor. The editor links to the stage's configuration page where you can make edits.

### AI generation button is disabled

The generation button requires both a repository and a branch to be selected before you can start. For private repositories, you also need at least one connected Git provider installation. If the repository dropdown shows "No repositories available" or "You need to connect a Git provider first", use the "Connect new Git provider" link in the editor to set up a [Git provider integration](/ci-cd-and-gitops/gitops-with-console).

### No repositories available for AI generation

For private repository generation, the editor pulls repositories from your connected Git provider installations. If the dropdown is empty despite having installations, check that the installation has access to the repository you need. The editor shows how many Git provider installations you have and provides a link to connect additional providers.

## Related features

- [Configuration files](/configuration/configuration-files) — TypeScript and YAML config formats and the `defineConfig` pattern.
- [AI config generation](/using-with-ai/config-generation-from-repository) — Deep dive into how the AI generator analyzes your repository.
- [GitOps with Console](/ci-cd-and-gitops/gitops-with-console) — Auto-deploy configs when you push to your repository.
- [Organizations, projects, and stages](/stacktape-console/organizations-projects-and-stages) — How projects and stages relate to configs.
- [Secrets](/configuration/secrets) — Reference secrets in your config with the `$Secret()` directive.
- [Connecting resources](/configuration/connecting-resources) — Use `connectTo` to wire resources together.

## FAQ

### Should I use the visual config editor or my local IDE?

Both work well at different stages. The config editor is convenient when you want to draft a config in the Console — for example, when starting from a preset or generating one from a Git repository. Once your project's config lives in version control, most teams edit `stacktape.ts` directly in their IDE using the [TypeScript config](/configuration/configuration-files) format, which gives you variables, conditionals, loops, and IDE refactoring tools.

### Can I generate a config from a private repository?

Yes. Choose the **Private** repository type in the AI generation dialog. The dropdown lists repositories from your connected Git provider installations. If no repositories appear, you need to connect a Git provider first — the editor provides a "Connect new Git provider" link to set up a [Git provider integration](/ci-cd-and-gitops/gitops-with-console).

### What is the difference between templates and config files?

Templates are named Stacktape configurations stored in the Console and edited in the visual config editor. A local project config file is either `stacktape.ts` or `stacktape.yaml`, used by the [CLI](/cli/deploy) for deployments. [GitOps](/ci-cd-and-gitops/gitops-with-console) is another deployment option that works from your repository. To move from a template to a deployable project, use the template content in a Stacktape config file in your project.

### Is the CloudFormation preview the exact template that gets deployed?

The CloudFormation preview is a static approximation. It shows what AWS resources Stacktape creates from your configuration, but it is not 100% accurate because some information is known only during deployment. The preview is useful for understanding your stack's structure before deployment, but the exact template used during deployment resolves all dynamic values.

### Can I edit a stage-linked config in the editor?

No — templates marked as created in the Console for a new stage are read-only in the config editor. The editor links to the stage's configuration page where you can make edits. See [organizations, projects, and stages](/stacktape-console/organizations-projects-and-stages) for more on how stages relate to configs.

### What happens if AI config generation fails?

If initialization fails, the Console shows "Failed to initialize AI config generation." The generation runs in a temporary Console project that is cleaned up automatically afterward, so a failed attempt leaves nothing behind — review your repository and branch selections and try again. See [AI config generation](/using-with-ai/config-generation-from-repository) for how the analysis works.

---

# Agent Mode in Dev

Source: docs/docs/using-with-ai/agent-mode-in-dev.mdx
Route: /using-with-ai/agent-mode-in-dev

# Agent Mode in Dev


> **Warning - Preview — in active development:** Agent mode is usable today but still evolving. Verify available endpoints by calling `GET /` on the running agent server. Some details on this page may change between Stacktape releases.


Stacktape [dev mode](/local-development/dev-mode-overview) can start an HTTP API server alongside your local workloads and databases. This **agent mode** gives AI coding assistants — and any HTTP client — programmatic access to check workload status, trigger rebuilds, read log file paths, query local databases, and call AWS SDK commands, enabling automated edit-test-fix loops without manual intervention.

## When to use

Agent mode is designed for workflows where an AI coding assistant drives the edit-test-fix loop. Enable it when:

- **AI-driven development** — You're using Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, or another AI coding assistant to build your application. The assistant starts `stacktape dev --agent` as a shell command, then uses the HTTP API to check status, rebuild workloads, read the log file path, query local databases, call AWS SDK commands, and stop dev mode.
- **Automated test harnesses** — You want a script to verify application behavior during development by hitting the status, env, or database endpoints.
- **Database inspection without client tools** — You need to run SQL queries, check Redis keys, scan DynamoDB items, or search OpenSearch indices against local dev databases without installing separate database clients.

## When NOT to use

- **Manual development** — If you're editing code yourself and checking results in a browser, standard [dev mode](/local-development/dev-mode-overview) without `--agent` is simpler. The TUI output is optimized for human reading.
- **Production observability** — Agent mode runs locally. For deployed stacks, use [`stacktape logs`](/cli/logs), [`stacktape metrics`](/cli/metrics), or the [Stacktape Console](/stacktape-console/console-overview).
- **CI/CD pipelines** — Use [`stacktape deploy`](/cli/deploy) directly in your pipeline. Agent mode is a development-time feature.

## Starting agent mode

Add the `--agent` flag to the [`stacktape dev`](/cli/dev) command to start the HTTP API server alongside your workloads.

```bash
stacktape dev --agent --agentPort 9900 --stage dev --region eu-west-1
```

| Flag            | Description                                                                              |
| --------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--agent`       | Enables the agent HTTP API server alongside dev mode                                     |
| `--agentPort`   | Port for the agent HTTP server                                                           |
| `--stage`       | Stage used for dev mode and the `.stacktape/dev-data/{stage}/` directory                 |
| `--region`      | AWS region used by dev mode and as the default for `/aws/sdk` when `region` is omitted   |
| `--resources`   | Which workloads to run (`all` or comma-separated names)                                  |
| `--freshDb`     | Reset local database data on startup                                                     |

Once ready, Stacktape prints the agent URL and a summary of available endpoints. Local database data persists in `.stacktape/dev-data/{stage}/` between runs — use `--freshDb` to wipe it for a clean slate.


> **Tip:** AI coding assistants can call `GET /` on the agent server to receive a full endpoint listing dynamically. Use `GET /?compact=true` for a shorter LLM-oriented response — it includes the current phase, workload names, available local resources with their primary query/schema endpoints, core action templates (rebuild, logs, env, aws, stop), and a pointer to `GET /` for full docs.


## Response format

Every agent endpoint wraps its result in a consistent JSON envelope so clients can handle success and error uniformly.

```json
{
  "v": "1.0",
  "ok": true,
  "code": "OK",
  "message": "OK",
  "data": {}
}
```

| Field     | Description                                                                          |
| --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `v`       | Envelope version (currently `"1.0"`)                                                 |
| `ok`      | `true` on success, `false` on error                                                  |
| `code`    | Machine-readable status. Common values include `OK`, `NOT_FOUND`, `VALIDATION_ERROR`, `INTERNAL_ERROR`, `ALREADY_EXISTS`, `AUTH_ERROR`, and `READ_ONLY_VIOLATION` |
| `message` | Human-readable description (optional — most responses include it, but not all)       |
| `data`    | Endpoint-specific payload (optional — present when the endpoint returns structured data) |

Many not-found and validation errors include contextual hints in `data`, such as an `available` array of valid resource names or a `hint` string describing expected input. For example, referencing a non-existent workload returns the list of available workloads in `data.available` and a usage hint in `data.hint`. This makes it easy for AI agents to self-correct without extra calls.

Agent endpoints return a JSON envelope with `v`, `ok`, and `code` on every response; most successful responses also include `message` and endpoint-specific `data`. Response examples throughout this page may omit envelope fields like `v` for brevity.

## Core endpoints

### Status

The `/status` endpoint reports the current agent phase and a map of workload names to their local URLs (if running) or status strings. AI agents should poll this endpoint after starting dev mode and wait for `"ready"` before testing workload URLs.

```bash
curl http://localhost:9900/status
```

```json
{
  "v": "1.0",
  "ok": true,
  "code": "OK",
  "data": {
    "phase": "ready",
    "ready": true,
    "workloads": {
      "api": "http://localhost:3000",
      "worker": "running"
    },
    "pid": 12345
  }
}
```

The `phase` value is one of `starting`, `ready`, `rebuilding`, `stopping`, or `stopped`. In the current server code, `/status` moves phase from `starting` to `ready` once workloads are running or errored, and `/stop` moves phase to `stopping`. The `ready` field becomes `true` when there is at least one workload and every workload has reached `running` or `error` status.

For detailed information including uptime, per-workload type/port/error data, local resource status, and the log file path, use verbose mode.

```bash
curl "http://localhost:9900/status?verbose=true"
```

The verbose response replaces the workload string map with an array of objects containing `name`, `type`, `status`, `url`, `port`, and `error` fields, plus a `localResources` array, `uptime` in seconds, and the `logFile` path. Workload `status` is one of `pending`, `starting`, `running`, `error`, or `stopped`. Local resource status uses the same values.

### Logs

The `/logs` endpoint returns the path to the JSONL log file on disk. Logs are **not** streamed through the HTTP response — AI agents should read the file directly using standard tools like `tail`, `grep`, or `jq`.

```bash
curl http://localhost:9900/logs
```

```json
{
  "v": "1.0",
  "ok": true,
  "code": "OK",
  "data": {
    "logFile": ".stacktape/dev-data/dev/9900-logs.txt",
    "format": "jsonl",
    "hint": "Use standard tools: tail -n 50 <logFile> | jq ."
  }
}
```

Each line in the log file is a JSON object with these fields:

| Field    | Example                      | Description                           |
| -------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------- |
| `ts`     | `"2025-03-15T14:30:15Z"`    | ISO timestamp                         |
| `source` | `"api"`                      | Workload name that produced the log   |
| `level`  | `"info"`, `"warn"`, `"error"` | Severity level                      |
| `msg`    | `"Server started on :3000"`  | Log message content                   |

View the last 50 lines:

```bash
tail -n 50 .stacktape/dev-data/dev/9900-logs.txt
```

Filter for errors only:

```bash
grep '"level":"error"' .stacktape/dev-data/dev/9900-logs.txt
```

Filter by a specific workload:

```bash
grep '"source":"api"' .stacktape/dev-data/dev/9900-logs.txt
```

This file-based approach is intentional — AI coding assistants already have file-reading tools, and `tail -f` provides real-time streaming without polling overhead.

### Environment variables

The `/env/{workload}` endpoint returns the environment variables available to a specific workload. For Lambda functions, variables are fetched from the deployed AWS function configuration. For other workload types whose env vars are tracked by dev mode (currently container, hosting-bucket, and nextjs-web workloads), variables are read from the local runner state.

```bash
curl http://localhost:9900/env/api
```

```json
{
  "v": "1.0",
  "ok": true,
  "code": "OK",
  "data": {
    "workload": "api",
    "type": "function",
    "source": "aws",
    "envVars": {
      "STP_NOTES_TABLE_TABLE_NAME": "my-project-dev-notesTable",
      "AWS_REGION": "eu-west-1"
    },
    "count": 2
  }
}
```

This endpoint is useful for debugging [`connectTo`](/configuration/connecting-resources) wiring. If an expected `STP_*` variable is missing, the workload's `connectTo` array likely doesn't reference the target resource. The response also includes the `source` field — `"aws"` for Lambda functions (fetched from the deployed function configuration) or `"local"` for other tracked workload types (currently container, hosting-bucket, and nextjs-web workloads, read from the local runner state).

### Rebuild

After editing application code, trigger a workload rebuild to pick up the changes. Rebuilding a specific workload is faster than rebuilding all of them — AI agents should target only the workload whose code changed.

Rebuild a specific workload:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/rebuild/api
```

Rebuild all workloads at once:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/rebuild/all
```

If a workload is currently in the `starting` state (mid-rebuild), the server rejects the request with an error message. The rebuild-all endpoint also rejects if any workload is mid-rebuild, listing the names of the workloads that are still starting.

### Liveness check

`GET /health` returns the current agent phase and can be used as a simple liveness check. Unlike `/status`, `/health` does not include workloads or local resources — it only returns `ok` and the current agent `phase` inside the standard envelope. Use `/status` for full workload details.

```bash
curl http://localhost:9900/health
```

### Resource health checks

Check the health of a specific local resource (database, cache). The agent monitors local resources automatically, but this endpoint triggers an immediate check and returns the result.

```bash
curl http://localhost:9900/health/myDb
```

The response includes `name`, `type`, `port`, `host`, and the `status` value returned by the local health monitor. If the resource name doesn't match any running local resource, the error response includes an `available` array listing valid resource names.

### Stop

Gracefully shut down dev mode, all workloads, and all local resources.

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/stop
```

Calling stop when the agent is already stopping or stopped returns success without side effects.

## Database endpoints

Agent mode exposes HTTP endpoints for local resource instances started by dev mode. The current server has typed handlers for PostgreSQL, MySQL/MariaDB, Redis, DynamoDB, and OpenSearch resources, so AI agents can inspect schemas, run queries, and verify data without installing separate database client tools. `GET /` lists only the database endpoint groups for local resources currently running in this dev session. If a resource is not running or has a different local type, the endpoint returns an error with available resource names where applicable.

The `{name}` path parameter in every database endpoint matches the resource name in your Stacktape configuration. If you reference a name that doesn't exist, the error response includes an `available` array listing the valid resource names.


> **Warning:** The PostgreSQL, MySQL, and Redis command/query endpoints accept an optional `confirm` field. The startup documentation warns that destructive operations require `{"confirm": true}` in the request body — without it, the request is rejected. The DynamoDB and OpenSearch delete handlers do not accept a `confirm` parameter; they require the key or document identifier fields shown below.


### PostgreSQL

Available when dev mode is running a local instance for a [relational database](/resources/databases/relational-database) with a PostgreSQL engine in your Stacktape configuration.

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/postgres/myDb/query \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"sql": "SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 10"}'
```

| Endpoint                          | Method | Description                    | Parameters                            |
| --------------------------------- | ------ | ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------- |
| `/postgres/{name}/query`          | POST   | Execute SQL                    | `{sql, limit?, timeout?, confirm?}`   |
| `/postgres/{name}/schema`         | GET    | Column types and constraints   | `?tables=t1,t2` (optional filter)     |
| `/postgres/{name}/tables`         | GET    | List all tables                |                                       |
| `/postgres/{name}/explain`        | POST   | Query execution plan           | `{sql, analyze?}`                     |
| `/postgres/{name}/indexes`        | GET    | Index definitions              | `?table=name` (optional filter)       |
| `/postgres/{name}/stats`          | GET    | Table statistics               | `?table=name` (optional filter)       |
| `/postgres/{name}/sample`         | GET    | Sample rows from a table       | `?table=name&limit=5`                 |

### MySQL

Available when dev mode is running a local instance for a [relational database](/resources/databases/relational-database) with a MySQL or MariaDB engine in your Stacktape configuration. The endpoint path uses `/mysql/` for both MySQL and MariaDB resources.

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/mysql/myDb/query \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"sql": "SHOW TABLES"}'
```

| Endpoint                        | Method | Description                    | Parameters                                             |
| ------------------------------- | ------ | ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `/mysql/{name}/query`           | POST   | Execute SQL                    | `{sql, limit?, timeout?, confirm?}`                    |
| `/mysql/{name}/schema`          | GET    | Column types and constraints   | `?tables=t1,t2` (optional filter)                      |
| `/mysql/{name}/tables`          | GET    | List all tables                |                                                        |
| `/mysql/{name}/explain`         | POST   | Query execution plan           | `{sql, format?}` (format: `"traditional"`, `"json"`, or `"tree"`) |
| `/mysql/{name}/indexes`         | GET    | Index definitions              | `?table=name` (optional filter)                        |
| `/mysql/{name}/stats`           | GET    | Table statistics               | `?table=name` (optional filter)                        |
| `/mysql/{name}/sample`          | GET    | Sample rows from a table       | `?table=name&limit=5`                                  |

### Redis

Available when dev mode is running a local instance for a [Redis cluster](/resources/databases/redis) in your Stacktape configuration. The `cmd` field accepts a Redis command as a single string (e.g. `"GET mykey"`, `"SET user:1 '{\"name\":\"Alice\"}'"`).

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/redis/myCache/command \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"cmd": "GET session:user123"}'
```

| Endpoint                       | Method | Description                  | Parameters                       |
| ------------------------------ | ------ | ---------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| `/redis/{name}/command`        | POST   | Execute any Redis command    | `{cmd, confirm?}`               |
| `/redis/{name}/keys`           | GET    | List keys matching pattern   | `?pattern=*&limit=100`          |
| `/redis/{name}/get`            | GET    | Get value by key             | `?key=name`                     |
| `/redis/{name}/ttl`            | GET    | Get TTL for a key            | `?key=name`                     |
| `/redis/{name}/info`           | GET    | Server info                  | `?section=memory` (optional)    |
| `/redis/{name}/type`           | GET    | Get type of a key            | `?key=name`                     |

### DynamoDB

Available when dev mode is running a local instance for a [DynamoDB table](/resources/databases/dynamodb) in your Stacktape configuration. Key parameters (`pk`, `sk`) are objects mapping attribute names to values — for example, `{"userId": "123"}`.

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/dynamodb/notesTable/scan \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"limit": 10}'
```

| Endpoint                           | Method | Description                  | Parameters                                    |
| ---------------------------------- | ------ | ---------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| `/dynamodb/{name}/query`           | POST   | Query by partition/sort key  | `{pk: {key: val}, sk?, index?, limit?}`       |
| `/dynamodb/{name}/scan`            | POST   | Full table scan              | `{filter?, limit?}`                           |
| `/dynamodb/{name}/get`             | POST   | Get single item              | `{pk: {key: val}, sk?}`                       |
| `/dynamodb/{name}/put`             | POST   | Put an item                  | `{item: {...}}`                               |
| `/dynamodb/{name}/delete`          | POST   | Delete an item               | `{pk: {key: val}, sk?}`                       |
| `/dynamodb/{name}/schema`          | GET    | Table key schema and indexes |                                                |
| `/dynamodb/{name}/sample`          | GET    | Sample items                 | `?limit=5`                                    |

### OpenSearch

Available when dev mode is running a local instance for an [OpenSearch domain](/resources/databases/opensearch) in your Stacktape configuration. Query bodies follow standard OpenSearch query DSL.

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/opensearch/searchDomain/search \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query": {"match": {"title": "serverless"}}, "limit": 10}'
```

| Endpoint                             | Method | Description                  | Parameters                           |
| ------------------------------------ | ------ | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `/opensearch/{name}/search`          | POST   | Search documents             | `{query: {...}, index?, limit?}`     |
| `/opensearch/{name}/get`             | GET    | Get document by ID           | `?index=idx&id=docid`               |
| `/opensearch/{name}/put`             | POST   | Index a document             | `{index, id?, doc: {...}}`           |
| `/opensearch/{name}/delete`          | POST   | Delete a document            | `{index, id}`                        |
| `/opensearch/{name}/indices`         | GET    | List all indices             |                                      |
| `/opensearch/{name}/mapping`         | GET    | Get index mapping            | `?index=idx`                         |
| `/opensearch/{name}/analyze`         | POST   | Analyze text with tokenizer  | `{text, analyzer?, index?}`          |
| `/opensearch/{name}/count`           | GET    | Count documents              | `?index=idx` (optional)              |

## AWS SDK access

Agent mode provides direct access to AWS SDK commands through the `/aws/sdk` endpoint. The endpoint uses credentials returned by `getDevAgentCredentials()` — dev agent role credentials scoped to the stack, with a fallback to user credentials. If no usable access key and secret key are returned, the request fails with an error. The startup message describes this access as scoped to stack resources via IAM role.

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:9900/aws/sdk \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"service": "lambda", "command": "ListFunctions", "input": {"MaxItems": 10}}'
```

The request body accepts `service` (required), `command` (required), `input` (optional, defaults to `{}`), and `region` (optional, defaults to the dev mode region). If `service` or `command` is missing, the agent returns a validation error with a hint showing the expected request shape.

To list all supported services and example commands:

```bash
curl http://localhost:9900/aws/sdk/services
```

`GET /aws/sdk/services` returns a successful envelope whose payload includes the supported services from `getSupportedServices()`. In the current implementation, the services payload is nested under `data.data` in the response. Use the running endpoint response as the authoritative shape and list of valid `service` values before constructing `/aws/sdk` calls. The service list in the startup message and on this page is a summary; the endpoint response is always up to date.

Common AWS SDK calls for AI agents:

| Use case                  | Service            | Command              | Example input                                                  |
| ------------------------- | ------------------ | -------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Read Lambda logs          | `logs`             | `FilterLogEvents`    | `{"logGroupName": "/aws/lambda/fn", "filterPattern": "ERROR"}` |
| Scan a DynamoDB table     | `dynamodb`         | `Scan`               | `{"TableName": "tbl", "Limit": 10}`                           |
| List S3 objects           | `s3`               | `ListObjectsV2`      | `{"Bucket": "bkt", "Prefix": "uploads/"}`                     |
| Invoke a Lambda           | `lambda`           | `Invoke`             | `{"FunctionName": "fn", "Payload": "{}"}`                     |
| Describe a stack          | `cloudformation`   | `DescribeStacks`     | `{"StackName": "my-stack"}`                                    |


> **Info:** The startup message lists supported `service` identifiers: `lambda`, `dynamodb`, `s3`, `logs`, `cloudformation`, `cloudwatch`, `sqs`, `sns`, `sfn`, `eventbridge`, `secretsmanager`, `ssm`, `sts`, `iam`, `ec2`, `ecs`, `ecr`, `rds`, `ses`, `cognito`, `apigatewayv2`, `xray`, `kinesis`, `firehose`, and more. Call `GET /aws/sdk/services` on the running agent for the complete, up-to-date list.


## Typical AI agent workflow

The core value of agent mode is the **automated feedback loop**: an AI agent writes code, checks if it works, reads any errors, fixes them, and rebuilds — all without human intervention.


## Flow
1. **Write config**: AI writes or edits stacktape.ts based on your requirements
2. **Start agent mode**: AI runs stacktape dev --agent to boot workloads and databases
3. **Write application code**: AI implements handlers, services, and frontend code
4. **Rebuild and test**: AI calls POST /rebuild/{name}, checks status, reads logs, queries databases
5. **Fix and iterate**: AI reads error logs, fixes code, rebuilds — repeating until everything works
6. **Deploy**: Once working locally, AI deploys to AWS with stacktape deploy


### Example session

An AI coding assistant building a notes API would follow this sequence:

1. Write `stacktape.ts` with a Lambda function, DynamoDB table, and HTTP API Gateway trigger
2. Start agent mode: `stacktape dev --agent --agentPort 9900 --stage dev --region eu-west-1`
3. Poll `GET /status` until `phase` is `"ready"` and `ready` is `true`
4. Write the handler code in `src/handler.ts`
5. Call `POST /rebuild/api` to pick up the new code
6. Test the endpoint by calling the workload URL from the status response
7. If errors occur, get the log file path from `GET /logs`, then read it: `tail -n 50 <logFile>`
8. Check `GET /env/api` to verify that `STP_NOTES_TABLE_TABLE_NAME` is injected
9. Query the database directly via `POST /dynamodb/notesTable/scan` to verify stored data
10. Fix code, rebuild, and test again until everything works
11. Deploy to AWS with `stacktape deploy`

Here is a minimal Stacktape configuration that an AI agent might produce for this workflow:


Example (TypeScript):

```typescript
import {
  defineConfig,
  LambdaFunction,
  StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging,
  DynamoDbTable,
  HttpApiGateway
} from 'stacktape';
export default defineConfig(() => {
  const notesTable = new DynamoDbTable({
    primaryKey: { partitionKey: { name: 'noteId', type: 'string' } }
  });

  const api = new LambdaFunction({
    packaging: new StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging({
      entryfilePath: './src/handler.ts'
    }),
    connectTo: [notesTable],
    events: [
      {
        type: 'http-api-gateway',
        properties: { httpApiGatewayName: 'apiGw', path: '/{proxy+}', method: '*' }
      }
    ]
  });

  const apiGw = new HttpApiGateway({});

  return {
    resources: { notesTable, api, apiGw }
  };
});
```


### Tips for AI agent integrations

- **Poll status before testing** — Wait for `"phase": "ready"` before making requests to workload URLs. Workloads may still be compiling during the `starting` phase.
- **Use compact docs** — Call `GET /?compact=true` at the start of a session to discover available endpoints with minimal token usage. The full `GET /` listing is more detailed but token-heavy.
- **Rebuild selectively** — `POST /rebuild/{name}` rebuilds only the named workload. Use it instead of `/rebuild/all` when you changed code in a single workload.
- **Check env vars early** — `GET /env/{workload}` reveals whether [`connectTo`](/configuration/connecting-resources) is wired correctly before you start debugging application code.
- **Read logs from the file** — The `/logs` endpoint returns a file path. Use `tail`, `grep`, or `jq` to filter the JSONL log file. Use `tail -f` for real-time streaming.
- **Inspect schemas before writing queries** — Call the `/schema` or `/tables` endpoint to discover table and column names before writing SQL or DynamoDB queries.
- **Use database endpoints for verification** — After writing data through your API, query the database directly to confirm it was stored correctly.

## MCP server integration

For MCP-based assistant setup, see the [Stacktape MCP server](/using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup) page. If your AI assistant supports the Model Context Protocol (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf), the MCP server provides a higher-level integration layer. Agent mode's HTTP API is the lower-level alternative that works with any tool capable of making HTTP requests.

## Security

Stacktape advertises the agent URL as `http://localhost:{port}`. The server sets `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: null` and returns `403` for OPTIONS preflight requests. The implementation comments describe this as blocking browser-based cross-origin requests while keeping curl, scripts, and local tools usable.

The `/aws/sdk` endpoint uses credentials returned by `getDevAgentCredentials()`. If no access key and secret key are available, the endpoint returns an error (`AWS credentials not available`). The startup message describes this access as scoped to stack resources via IAM role. Treat the endpoint as having whatever permissions those credentials grant — avoid exposing the agent port to untrusted networks.

## FAQ

### Should I use agent mode or the Stacktape MCP server?

Both work with assistants like Claude Code, Cursor, and Windsurf. Agent mode is a plain HTTP API that any tool can call with `curl` or HTTP libraries — no protocol integration needed, so it suits custom tooling or any client that can make HTTP requests. The [MCP server](/using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup) uses the Model Context Protocol standard that those assistants support natively, providing a more integrated, higher-level layer. If your AI tool supports MCP, prefer the MCP server; reach for agent mode when you need raw HTTP access.

### What databases can I query through the agent API?

Agent mode exposes typed HTTP endpoints for five local resource types started by dev mode — PostgreSQL, MySQL/MariaDB, Redis, DynamoDB, and OpenSearch — so agents can inspect schemas, run queries, and verify data without installing separate database client tools. Each type has its own endpoint group (SQL engines get `query`/`schema`/`tables`/`explain`/`indexes`/`stats`/`sample`, plus key-value and document operations for Redis, DynamoDB, and OpenSearch). `GET /` lists only the endpoint groups for resources currently running in the session.

### Is my data persisted between agent mode sessions?

Yes. The startup message confirms local database data is stored in `.stacktape/dev-data/{stage}/` and survives restarts. Use the `--freshDb` flag when starting dev mode to reset all local databases to a clean state.

### Are destructive database operations safe during AI sessions?

The PostgreSQL, MySQL, and Redis query/command endpoints accept an optional `confirm` field. The startup documentation warns that destructive operations require `{"confirm": true}` — without it, the request is rejected. DynamoDB and OpenSearch delete handlers do not accept a `confirm` parameter; they require the key or document identifier fields directly.

### What happens if a workload fails to start?

The agent server keeps running even if individual workloads fail. The `/status` endpoint reports per-workload status — in verbose mode, each workload includes `status`, `url`, `port`, and `error` fields. AI agents can read the error, fix the code, and call `POST /rebuild/{name}` to retry without restarting the entire dev session.

### Why does an `/aws/sdk` call fail or return access-denied?

The `/aws/sdk` endpoint runs against dev agent credentials, which the source describes as scoped to the stack's IAM role (falling back to user credentials). Those credentials determine what resources are reachable — a call can fail with `AWS credentials not available` if no usable key pair is returned, or with an access-denied error if the role lacks permission for that resource. The endpoint supports a broad set of services (`lambda`, `dynamodb`, `s3`, `logs`, `cloudformation`, `sqs`, `sns`, `sfn`, and more); call `GET /aws/sdk/services` for the authoritative, up-to-date list of valid `service` values before constructing a call.

---

# AI Coding Assistant Integrations

Source: docs/docs/using-with-ai/ai-coding-assistant-integrations.mdx
Route: /using-with-ai/ai-coding-assistant-integrations

# AI Coding Assistant Integrations


> **Warning:** **Preview — in active development.** The Stacktape MCP server is under active development. Setup and behavior may change between Stacktape CLI versions. Expect improvements and possible breaking changes.


Stacktape integrates with AI coding assistants through the [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io). The [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add) command configures Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code with GitHub Copilot, Windsurf, OpenAI Codex, and OpenCode to launch Stacktape's local MCP server. The generated config tells the assistant to run `stacktape mcp` as a local MCP server process.

## When to use

Use the MCP integration when your AI coding assistant supports MCP and you want it to launch Stacktape's local MCP server directly from the editor. See the [MCP server setup](/using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup) page for the current tool list and server behavior. The integration is most valuable when you want a tighter feedback loop between asking questions in your assistant and taking action on your Stacktape project.

## When NOT to use

Skip MCP setup if you prefer running Stacktape commands in a terminal, or if your AI client does not support MCP. For operations not covered by the MCP server, use the [CLI](/cli/deploy) directly. See [MCP server setup](/using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup) for the current tool list.

## Supported assistants

The [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add) command checks known config-file paths and writes the correct MCP configuration for the clients you select. Six AI coding assistants are supported:

| Assistant | Config format | Root key | Project config path | Global config path |
|-----------|--------------|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Claude Code | JSON | `mcpServers` | `.mcp.json` | `~/.claude.json` |
| Cursor | JSON | `mcpServers` | `.cursor/mcp.json` | `~/.cursor/mcp.json` |
| VS Code / GitHub Copilot | JSON | `servers` | `.vscode/mcp.json` | Platform-specific `User/mcp.json` |
| Windsurf | JSON | `mcpServers` | `.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json` | `~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json` |
| OpenAI Codex | TOML | `mcp_servers` | `.codex/config.toml` | `~/.codex/config.toml` |
| OpenCode | JSON/JSONC | `mcp` | `opencode.jsonc` or `.opencode/config.json` | Platform-specific (see below) |

VS Code global paths vary by platform: on Windows `%APPDATA%\Code\User\mcp.json`, on macOS `~/Library/Application Support/Code/User/mcp.json`, on Linux `~/.config/Code/User/mcp.json`.

Windsurf global path resolves to the user's home directory on all platforms: `~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json` (on Windows, `%USERPROFILE%\.codeium\windsurf\mcp_config.json`).

OpenCode global paths vary by platform: on Windows `%APPDATA%\opencode\opencode.jsonc` or `%APPDATA%\opencode\config.json`; on macOS and Linux `~/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc`, `~/.config/opencode/config.json`, or `~/.opencode/config.json`.

Generated configs launch `stacktape mcp` locally. Claude Code, Cursor, and VS Code configs explicitly set `type: 'stdio'`; Windsurf omits the type field; OpenCode uses `type: 'local'` with the command as an array; Codex uses TOML with no type field. Other MCP-compatible clients may work if they can launch a local command with arguments and environment variables, but `mcp:add` only writes configs for the six listed clients.

## One-command setup

The [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add) command configures Stacktape MCP for one or more supported AI coding assistants in a single invocation. When run interactively, it presents a prompt where you select which clients to configure, then writes the correct MCP server entry to each client's config file. In agent mode or with `--autoConfirmOperation`, the prompt is skipped and the default selection is used. Run it in your project directory:

```bash
stacktape mcp:add
```

When prompting is available, the interactive prompt lists the supported clients. Clients whose config file already exists on disk are labeled `(detected)` and pre-selected. Select the clients you want, confirm, and the command creates or updates the `stacktape` MCP server entry in each client's config file.

Before modifying any existing file, `mcp:add` creates a timestamped `.bak` backup (e.g. `.mcp.json.bak.2025-03-15T10-30-00-000Z`). If something goes wrong, restore from the backup.

### Non-interactive mode

To skip the interactive prompt (useful in CI or scripted setups), pass `--autoConfirmOperation`. The prompt is also skipped automatically in agent mode.

```bash
stacktape mcp:add --autoConfirmOperation
```

The default selection is all detected clients. If no existing config files are detected, the default selection is all six clients. With `--autoConfirmOperation`, that default selection is used without prompting.

### How detection works

Detection checks whether a config file already exists at any of the candidate paths for a given client — it does not check whether the client application is installed. A freshly installed client that has never been opened will not have a config file yet. Select it manually from the interactive prompt, or configure it by hand using the examples in [Manual setup](#manual-setup).

### Local vs global Stacktape command

When your project lists `stacktape` as a dependency in `package.json` (in `dependencies`, `devDependencies`, `optionalDependencies`, or `peerDependencies`), `mcp:add` writes `node_modules/.bin/stacktape` as the command for project-level config files. Global config files always use the `stacktape` command. This ensures the MCP server uses your project's pinned Stacktape version for local configs and the globally installed version for global configs.

### Project-local vs global config

The `mcp:add` command checks project-level config paths first, then global paths. It writes to the first existing file it finds for each client. If no config file exists for a client, it creates a new project-level config file at the first candidate path.

## What the MCP server provides

The [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add) command writes config that tells the AI assistant to run `stacktape mcp` as a local MCP server process. Separately, the Stacktape Console hosts an HTTP-based MCP endpoint (`POST /mcp`) using Streamable HTTP transport for web-based integrations.

For the current tool list and server behavior, see [MCP server setup](/using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup).

## Manual setup

Stacktape MCP can be configured manually by adding a `stacktape` server entry to your AI coding assistant's MCP config file. Use manual setup when you prefer not to run [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add), or when configuring a client that `mcp:add` does not support. The examples below show the config structure that `mcp:add` generates for each of the six supported clients.


> **Info:** The `mcp:add` command includes a `STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME` environment variable in generated configs. If you configure a client manually, include the same variable to match the generated setup.


### Tab: Claude Code


Add to `.mcp.json` in your project root or `~/.claude.json` globally:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "stacktape": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "stacktape",
      "args": ["mcp"],
      "env": {
        "STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME": "claude-code"
      }
    }
  }
}
```


### Tab: Cursor


Add to `.cursor/mcp.json` in your project root or `~/.cursor/mcp.json` globally:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "stacktape": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "stacktape",
      "args": ["mcp"],
      "env": {
        "STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME": "cursor"
      }
    }
  }
}
```


### Tab: VS Code / Copilot


Add to `.vscode/mcp.json` in your project root or your VS Code user-level `mcp.json`. VS Code uses `servers` as the root key, not `mcpServers`:

```json
{
  "servers": {
    "stacktape": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "stacktape",
      "args": ["mcp"],
      "env": {
        "STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME": "vscode"
      }
    }
  }
}
```


### Tab: Windsurf


Add to `.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json` in your project root or `~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json` globally. Windsurf configs omit the `type` field:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "stacktape": {
      "command": "stacktape",
      "args": ["mcp"],
      "env": {
        "STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME": "windsurf"
      }
    }
  }
}
```


### Tab: OpenAI Codex


Add to `.codex/config.toml` in your project root or `~/.codex/config.toml` globally:

```toml
[mcp_servers.stacktape]
command = "stacktape"
args = ["mcp"]
enabled = true

[mcp_servers.stacktape.env]
STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME = "codex"
```


### Tab: OpenCode


Add to `opencode.jsonc` or `.opencode/config.json` in your project root, or to the global config path for your platform. OpenCode uses `mcp` as the root key, `"type": "local"`, and passes the command as an array:

```json
{
  "mcp": {
    "stacktape": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["stacktape", "mcp"],
      "enabled": true,
      "env": {
        "STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME": "opencode"
      }
    }
  }
}
```


> **Tip:** If your project has `stacktape` as an npm dependency, replace `"stacktape"` with `"node_modules/.bin/stacktape"` in project-level configs to pin the MCP server to your project's Stacktape version.


## Verifying the integration

After configuring Stacktape MCP with [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add) or manual setup, verify the integration is working by opening your AI assistant and asking a Stacktape-related question. If the client reads the config file, it should run `stacktape mcp` as the configured local MCP server. If the assistant does not appear to use Stacktape context, check the following:

1. **The `stacktape` command is on your PATH.** Run `stacktape --version` in a terminal to confirm.
2. **The `mcp` subcommand exists.** Older Stacktape CLI versions may not include it. Run [`stacktape upgrade`](/cli/upgrade) to get the latest version.
3. **The config file has correct syntax.** For JSON configs, validate the file parses without errors. For JSONC files (OpenCode), ensure comments use `//` syntax. The `mcp:add` command creates `.bak` backups before modifying files, so you can restore if needed.
4. **Your AI client has MCP enabled.** Some clients require toggling MCP support in their settings before they read MCP config files.
5. **The config file is in the right location.** Some clients only read project-level configs when the project is open as a workspace. If in doubt, use a global config path.

## Keeping the integration up to date

Generated configs run whichever `stacktape` binary the config points to. Updating that binary — via [`stacktape upgrade`](/cli/upgrade) or updating the `stacktape` npm package — means the next MCP server launch uses the updated version automatically.

Re-run [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add) only if you want to change which clients are configured or switch between project-level and global configs. The command is idempotent — running it again against an already-configured client leaves the config unchanged (status: `unchanged`) and does not create a backup.

## FAQ

### What is MCP and why does Stacktape use it?

[MCP (Model Context Protocol)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io) is an open standard for connecting AI assistants to external tools. For these coding-assistant integrations, the generated config tells the assistant to run `stacktape mcp` as a local MCP server. MCP can also be served over HTTP transports — the Stacktape Console hosts a separate HTTP-based MCP endpoint for web-based integrations.

### Which AI coding assistants work with Stacktape?

Six clients have automatic setup via [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add): Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code with GitHub Copilot, Windsurf, OpenAI Codex, and OpenCode. Other MCP-compatible clients may work if they can launch a local command with arguments and environment variables; `mcp:add` only writes configs for the six listed clients.

### Should I use project-local or global config?

Use project-level config (`.mcp.json`, `.cursor/mcp.json`, etc.) when you want the MCP server pinned to your project's Stacktape version via `node_modules/.bin/stacktape`. Use global config (`~/.claude.json`, `~/.cursor/mcp.json`, etc.) when you want Stacktape MCP available across all projects using your globally installed CLI. The `mcp:add` command writes to the first existing file it finds for each client and creates project-level files when no config exists.

### Does `mcp:add` modify my existing config files?

Yes. The command reads your existing config, adds or updates the `stacktape` server entry under the appropriate root key (e.g. `mcpServers`, `servers`, or `mcp`), and writes the file back. It creates a timestamped `.bak` backup before any modification. If the config already has a `stacktape` entry with identical content, the file is left unchanged and no backup is created.

### My assistant isn't using Stacktape context — what's wrong?

Most failures come from a few causes: the `stacktape` command isn't on your PATH (run `stacktape --version` to check), your CLI is too old to include the `mcp` subcommand (run [`stacktape upgrade`](/cli/upgrade)), MCP support isn't enabled in your client's settings, or the config file is in a location the client doesn't read for the current workspace. See [Verifying the integration](#verifying-the-integration) for the full checklist.

### How do I remove the MCP configuration?

To remove the MCP configuration, open the config file for your client and delete the `stacktape` entry. For JSON configs, remove the `"stacktape": { ... }` object under the appropriate root key (`mcpServers`, `servers`, or `mcp`). For Codex TOML, remove the `[mcp_servers.stacktape]` section and its `[mcp_servers.stacktape.env]` subsection.

### How does MCP compare to using the Stacktape CLI directly?

The MCP integration lets your AI assistant run Stacktape's MCP server as a local process and exchange structured messages over the MCP protocol. This keeps the interaction inside your editor conversation rather than requiring you to copy-paste terminal output. The CLI gives you direct control over every flag and argument and is the primary interface for all Stacktape operations. See [MCP server setup](/using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup) for which operations the MCP server currently supports.

---

# AI Config Generation

Source: docs/docs/using-with-ai/config-generation-from-repository.mdx
Route: /using-with-ai/config-generation-from-repository

# AI Config Generation


> **Info:** The Console's AI config generator is in active development. The workflow described below reflects the current implementation, but details may change as the feature evolves.


The Stacktape Console includes an AI config generator that creates a starting Stacktape configuration from an existing Git repository. Select a repository and branch, run the generator, and then save the resulting configuration to a template in the [visual config editor](/stacktape-console/visual-config-editor) for review and editing.


> **Info:** This page covers the Console's AI config generator. Stacktape also integrates with AI coding assistants through the [MCP server](/using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup) and supports [Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, and other tools](/using-with-ai/ai-coding-assistant-integrations). See the [AI workflows overview](/using-with-ai/overview) for a comparison of all AI-assisted paths.


## When to use

AI Config Generation gives you a starting Stacktape config from an existing codebase, so you can review and edit instead of writing one from scratch. Use it when:

- **You have an existing project** and want Stacktape to infer the right resources rather than writing a config from scratch.
- **You're evaluating Stacktape** and want to see what a real config looks like for your codebase, not for a generic starter project.
- **You want a first config draft from an existing repository** — run the generator, then review the generated resources and wiring yourself rather than writing everything from scratch.

## When NOT to use

AI Config Generation is not the right fit for every project. Skip it when the overhead of reviewing a generated config outweighs writing one directly.

- **Starting from scratch** — if you don't have a codebase yet, use [`stacktape init`](/cli/init) to scaffold from a starter project instead. Starters come with both code and a matching config.
- **Simple single-function projects** — writing a config for one Lambda function takes less time than running the generator. See the [configuration guide](/getting-started/configure-your-stack).
- **You need full control from the start** — the generator produces a starting point. If you already know your exact infrastructure requirements, writing the config directly gives you precise control without a review step.

## How it works

When you start generation, the Console creates a temporary project for the selected repository and branch, then invokes the AI generation pipeline. Input controls are disabled while the pipeline is running. Once generation completes, the temporary project is deleted automatically. You can then save the resulting configuration to the selected template in the config editor.

## Using the generator

AI Config Generation runs inside the Config Editor page in the [Stacktape Console](/stacktape-console/console-overview). You select a template, choose a repository type (public or private), provide a repository and branch, then start the generation run. The steps below walk through each part of this flow.

### Open the generation modal

Navigate to the **Config Editor** page in the [Stacktape Console](/stacktape-console/console-overview). After selecting a template, the Config Editor receives AI generation controls. The generator flow asks for a repository type, repository, and branch before it starts.

### Select a repository type

Inside the generation interface, choose whether to generate from a **public** or **private** repository using the toggle at the top.

**Public repositories** — enter the full Git URL directly into the "Repository URL" field (e.g., `https://github.com/your-org/your-repo`). No Git provider connection is needed.

**Private repositories** — select from repositories available through your connected Git provider installations. If no providers are connected, the dropdown shows "You need to connect a Git provider first." Click **Connect new Git provider** in the Console to set up a [Git provider integration](/ci-cd-and-gitops/gitops-with-console). Once a Git provider is connected, repositories available to Stacktape appear in the dropdown. If none are available, the dropdown shows "No repositories available."

### Select a branch

Pick the branch you want to generate from using the **Branch** dropdown. When the branch list contains a default branch, the Console auto-selects it. Choose a feature branch if it contains infrastructure changes you want reflected in the generated config. The dropdown shows "Please select a git repository first" until a repository is provided.

### Start generation

Start the generation process. The start control is disabled until both a repository and a branch are selected — the tooltip reads "Please select Git repository and branch to generate config from." Once generation starts, all input controls (repository type, URL, branch) are disabled while the pipeline runs.

### Review and save

When generation finishes, you can edit the resulting configuration in the config editor and save it to the selected template. Templates created for a new stage may be read-only in the Config Editor — if so, edit the config from the stage configuration page instead.


> **Info:** Treat generated output as a draft. Before deploying, review resource choices and add any project-specific settings such as secrets, domains, and sizing.


## After generation

The items below are general Stacktape deployment best practices — they are not specific to the AI generator, but they apply to any generated config you plan to deploy:

1. **Review resource types** — confirm each resource maps to the correct Stacktape resource type. For example, verify that an API is a [Lambda function](/resources/compute/lambda-function) vs. a [web service](/resources/compute/web-service), or that a frontend uses the right SSR resource ([Next.js](/resources/frontend/nextjs), [Nuxt](/resources/frontend/nuxt), [Astro](/resources/frontend/astro), etc.).

2. **Add secrets** — use [`$Secret()`](/configuration/directives) directives to reference secrets stored in the [Stacktape Console](/configuration/secrets). Never hard-code sensitive values in the config.

3. **Configure custom domains** — add [custom domains](/resources/networking/custom-domains) for production-facing services.

4. **Adjust resource sizing** — review memory, CPU, timeout, and scaling settings for each resource. Defaults may not match your workload requirements.

5. **Set up CI/CD** — connect [GitOps](/ci-cd-and-gitops/gitops-with-console) for automated deployments on push and PR previews.

6. **Production hardening** — add alarms, budgets, and guardrails. See [Going to production](/getting-started/going-to-production) for the full checklist.

## Tips for better results

Before running generation, make sure the selected branch contains the application code and build files you expect Stacktape to inspect. A clean, up-to-date branch gives the generator the best starting point — stale or incomplete code on the branch can lead to a config that doesn't reflect your project's current state.

## FAQ

### How is this different from `stacktape init`?

[`stacktape init`](/cli/init) scaffolds a new project from a predefined starter template — it creates boilerplate code and a matching config. AI Config Generation analyzes an **existing** codebase and produces a config tailored to what's already in the repository. Use `init` when starting fresh; use AI Config Generation when you already have code to deploy.

### Do I need to connect a Git provider for public repositories?

No. For public repositories, paste the Git URL directly into the URL input field. A Git provider connection is only required for private repositories where authentication is needed to clone the code.

### Why can't I edit the generated config in the editor?

You can normally edit the generated config in the [visual config editor](/stacktape-console/visual-config-editor) and save it to the selected template. However, templates created for a new stage may be read-only in the Config Editor — in that case, edit the config from the stage configuration page instead.

### What if generation fails?

If initialization fails, the Console shows "Failed to initialize AI config generation." Check that the repository URL, selected repository, and branch are correct, then try again.

### Is my repository data safe?

The generation pipeline runs on Stacktape's infrastructure. A temporary project is created for the selected repository and branch, then deleted automatically once generation completes.

### Does the generator produce a production-ready config?

No. Treat any generated config as a starting point: review resource choices, secrets, domains, sizing, alarms, budgets, and CI/CD before deploying. See [Going to production](/getting-started/going-to-production) for the complete production-readiness checklist.

---

# MCP Server Setup

Source: docs/docs/using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup.mdx
Route: /using-with-ai/mcp-server-setup

# MCP Server Setup


> **Warning:** Preview — in active development. The Stacktape MCP server is under active development. Some operations may not work end-to-end in all environments, and behavior may change as the server stabilizes.


The Stacktape MCP server gives AI coding assistants structured access to Stacktape documentation, CLI commands, project inspection, and dev mode control. Instead of copying CLI output back and forth, your assistant calls Stacktape tools directly and receives typed JSON responses it can reason about.

## When to use

Use the MCP server when your AI coding assistant supports the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io). The structured tool interface is better than raw CLI output for several workflows:

- **Deploying or deleting stacks** — the assistant plans the command, validates arguments against CLI metadata, and executes with safety gates, all through tool calls.
- **Debugging** — query [logs](/cli/logs), [metrics](/cli/metrics), [alarms](/cli/alarms), [database contents](/cli/query-sql), or [DynamoDB items](/cli/query-dynamodb) during a conversation. The assistant gets structured JSON instead of parsing terminal output.
- **Configuration help** — the assistant searches Stacktape's bundled documentation index for resource types, config syntax, and deployment patterns without opening a browser.
- **Dev mode** — start, monitor, rebuild, and stop [dev mode](/local-development/dev-mode-overview) sessions from within your AI conversation.

## When NOT to use

Skip the MCP server if your AI client does not support the Model Context Protocol — use the [Stacktape CLI](/cli/deploy) directly or the [Stacktape Console](/stacktape-console/console-overview) instead. For long-running deployments, prefer the CLI when you want to watch the command directly in your own terminal. MCP command responses are consolidated and may be truncated. The MCP server runs CLI commands as subprocesses and returns a consolidated response; if an optional caller-specified timeout is exceeded, the subprocess is terminated and an error is returned.

## Supported clients

[`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add) detects and configures six MCP clients automatically:

| Client | Project config path | Global config path | Config key |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|------------|
| Claude Code | `.mcp.json` | `~/.claude.json` | `mcpServers` |
| Cursor | `.cursor/mcp.json` | `~/.cursor/mcp.json` | `mcpServers` |
| VS Code / Copilot | `.vscode/mcp.json` | Platform-specific `User/mcp.json` | `servers` |
| Windsurf | `.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json` | `~/.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json` | `mcpServers` |
| OpenAI Codex | `.codex/config.toml` | `~/.codex/config.toml` | TOML section |
| OpenCode | `opencode.jsonc` or `.opencode/config.json` | Platform-specific (see below) | `mcp` |

OpenCode global paths: on Windows, `%APPDATA%\opencode\opencode.jsonc` or `%APPDATA%\opencode\config.json`. On macOS/Linux, `~/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc`, `~/.config/opencode/config.json`, or `~/.opencode/config.json`.

The MCP server uses stdio transport — every client launches `stacktape mcp` as a child process and communicates over stdin/stdout using JSON-RPC.


> **Info:** Detection is based on whether a config file exists on disk, not whether the client application is installed. A freshly installed client that has never been opened (and therefore has no config file) will not show the `(detected)` label in the interactive prompt — but you can still select it, and `mcp:add` will create the project-level config file.


## Automatic setup

Run [`stacktape mcp:add`](/cli/mcp-add) from your project directory:

```bash
stacktape mcp:add
```

The interactive prompt lists all six supported clients. Clients with an existing config file are labeled `(detected)` and pre-selected. Select the ones you want and confirm. `mcp:add` checks each client's known paths in order (project-level first, then global), writes to the first existing file, and creates the first project-level file when none exist. Existing config files are backed up with a `.bak.<timestamp>` suffix before modification.

To skip the interactive prompt and install into all detected clients (or all six if none are detected), pass `--autoConfirmOperation`:

```bash
stacktape mcp:add --autoConfirmOperation
```

When the project `package.json` lists `stacktape` in `dependencies`, `devDependencies`, `optionalDependencies`, or `peerDependencies`, `mcp:add` uses `node_modules/.bin/stacktape` for project-level configs and the global `stacktape` command for global configs. This pins the MCP server to your project's Stacktape version.

## Manual setup

If you prefer to configure the MCP server by hand, point your client at `stacktape mcp` using stdio transport. The snippets below are minimal manual configs. `mcp:add` also writes `STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME`; add it manually only if you want client tracking to match installer output.


### Tab: Claude Code


Add to `.mcp.json` in your project root or `~/.claude.json` globally:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "stacktape": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "stacktape",
      "args": ["mcp"]
    }
  }
}
```


### Tab: Cursor


Add to `.cursor/mcp.json` in your project root or `~/.cursor/mcp.json` globally:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "stacktape": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "stacktape",
      "args": ["mcp"]
    }
  }
}
```


### Tab: VS Code / Copilot


Add to `.vscode/mcp.json` in your project root or your VS Code user-level `mcp.json`. Note that VS Code uses the `servers` key instead of `mcpServers`:

```json
{
  "servers": {
    "stacktape": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "stacktape",
      "args": ["mcp"]
    }
  }
}
```


### Tab: Windsurf


Add to `.codeium/windsurf/mcp_config.json` in your project root or home directory:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "stacktape": {
      "command": "stacktape",
      "args": ["mcp"]
    }
  }
}
```


### Tab: OpenAI Codex


Add to `.codex/config.toml` in your project root or `~/.codex/config.toml` globally:

```toml
[mcp_servers.stacktape]
command = "stacktape"
args = ["mcp"]
enabled = true
```


### Tab: OpenCode


Add to `opencode.jsonc` or `.opencode/config.json` in your project root, or the platform-specific global path:

```json
{
  "mcp": {
    "stacktape": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["stacktape", "mcp"],
      "enabled": true
    }
  }
}
```


> **Info:** When you use `stacktape mcp:add`, the generated config also includes an `env` field with `STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME` set to the client identifier (e.g. `claude-code`, `cursor`, `vscode`). This is used for client tracking. Manual configurations can include the same field — for example, `"env": { "STACKTAPE_MCP_CLIENT_NAME": "claude-code" }` — but it is not required for the server to function.


> **Tip:** If your project has `stacktape` as an npm dependency, replace `"stacktape"` with `"node_modules/.bin/stacktape"` in project-level configs. This pins the MCP server to your project's Stacktape version instead of using the global install.


## Available tools

The MCP server exposes four tools. Each tool uses an `action` parameter to group related operations, keeping the tool surface small while giving assistants access to docs, project inspection, CLI commands, and dev mode control.

| Tool | Actions | Purpose |
|------|---------|---------|
| `stacktape_docs` | `search`, `get` | Search or fetch Stacktape documentation by query, route, resource type, definition name, property name, or heading path |
| `stacktape_project` | `scan`, `orient` | Inspect the local workspace for Stacktape config files, package scripts, dependencies, and suggested CLI defaults |
| `stacktape_cli` | `list`, `describe`, `plan`, `run` | Discover CLI commands, validate arguments, build execution plans, and execute non-interactive commands through safety-classified gates |
| `stacktape_dev` | `plan`, `start`, `status`, `logs`, `rebuild`, `rebuild_all`, `stop` | Plan and control [dev mode](/local-development/dev-mode-overview) sessions |

Tool arguments accept common snake_case and kebab-case forms (`project_name`, `config_path`, `start_time`, `end_time`, `secret_name`, etc.) and normalize them to camelCase automatically. Your AI assistant does not need to know the exact casing convention.

### Docs tool

`stacktape_docs` searches a documentation index bundled with the Stacktape CLI — no network access required. Use `action: "search"` with a `query` to find configuration syntax, resource types, deployment patterns, or troubleshooting guidance. Use `action: "get"` with a `route`, `definitionName`, `propertyName`, `sourcePath`, or `headingPath` to fetch an exact section.

Search supports three modes: `answer` (default) returns a synthesized answer with references, `reference` returns matched references without content, and `snippet` returns code-focused excerpts.

Key selectors and limits:

| Parameter | Actions | Type | Description |
|-----------|---------|------|-------------|
| `query` | `search` | string | Search query (required for `search`). |
| `mode` | `search` | `answer` \| `reference` \| `snippet` | Response mode. Default: `answer`. |
| `maxItems` | `search` | number | Max search results. Default: 3. |
| `docKind` | both | `docs-page` \| `config-reference` | Filter to a specific docs artifact kind. |
| `resourceType` | both | string | Filter or select by resource type, e.g. `function`. |
| `route` | `get` | string | Exact docs route, e.g. `/config-reference/function`. |
| `definitionName` | `get` | string | Exact TypeScript definition name from config reference. |
| `propertyName` | `get` | string | Exact config property name to extract. |
| `sourcePath` | `get` | string | Exact source path from a previous docs reference. |
| `headingPath` | `get` | string[] | Exact heading path array from a previous docs reference. |
| `maxChars` | `get` | number | Max content characters. Default: 16,000. |
| `includeFullPage` | `get` | boolean | Set `true` to fetch all content for a long route. |

For long route-only `get` requests, when more than 20 docs-page sections match and `includeFullPage` is not set, the server returns available heading paths instead of the full route content — the assistant passes one back to get the specific section.

### Project tool

`stacktape_project` with `action: "scan"` discovers Stacktape config candidates (`.ts`, `.js`, `.yml`, `.json`), parses `package.json` scripts that invoke Stacktape commands, detects lockfiles, and returns suggested defaults for `configPath` and `currentWorkingDirectory`. This is the first tool an assistant should call when entering an unfamiliar Stacktape project.

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|-----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `cwd` | string | Server process cwd | Absolute path to the Stacktape project root. Pass this whenever the assistant knows it. |
| `maxFiles` | number | 8 (`orient`: 12) | Maximum ranked files to return per category. |
| `includeDetails` | boolean | `false` | When `true`, includes full package script commands and unabridged candidate metadata. |

The `orient` action returns the same scan data with stronger next-action guidance for the assistant.

### CLI tool

`stacktape_cli` covers the non-interactive CLI surface: [deployments](/cli/deploy), [deletions](/cli/delete), [previewing changes](/cli/diff), [rollbacks](/cli/rollback), diagnostics ([`logs`](/cli/logs), [`metrics`](/cli/metrics), [`alarms`](/cli/alarms), [`query:sql`](/cli/query-sql), [`query:dynamodb`](/cli/query-dynamodb), [`query:redis`](/cli/query-redis), [`query:opensearch`](/cli/query-opensearch)), [secrets](/configuration/secrets) (`secret:set`, `secret:get`, `secret:delete`), parameters (`param:get`), stack info (`info:stack`, `info:stacks`), and deployment scripts (`script:run`).

The typical workflow:

1. **`action: "list"`** — discover available commands, optionally filtered by `category` or `safety` level.
2. **`action: "describe"`** — get full metadata for a specific command (allowed arguments, safety class, confirmation policy).
3. **`action: "plan"`** — build a read-only execution plan. Pass `cwd` with the absolute path to the Stacktape project root when the assistant has it — `plan` uses it to scan config files and `package.json` scripts. The action infers defaults, validates arguments against CLI metadata, and returns a suggested `action: "run"` payload with a shell command preview. No credentials are required for planning.
4. **`action: "run"`** — execute the planned command as a CLI subprocess. Mutating commands require `confirm: true`. Destructive commands require direct user confirmation through MCP form elicitation (see [Safety model](#safety-model)).

Common parameters accepted by `plan` and `run`:

| Parameter | Type | Description |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| `command` | string | CLI command name, e.g. `deploy`, `logs`, `secret:get` |
| `cwd` | string | Absolute path to the Stacktape project root for project scanning |
| `args` | object | CLI arguments as a camelCase key-value object |
| `stage` | string | Target Stacktape stage |
| `region` | string | Target AWS region |
| `projectName` | string | Stacktape project name |
| `awsAccount` | string | Connected Stacktape AWS account name |
| `configPath` | string | Stacktape config path relative to `currentWorkingDirectory` |
| `currentWorkingDirectory` | string | Working directory for resolving config and app files |
| `resourceName` | string | Resource name for diagnostics commands |
| `scriptName` | string | Deployment script name for `script:run` |
| `secretName` | string | Secret name for secret commands |
| `secretValue` | string | Secret value for `secret:set` (masked in tool output) |
| `secretFile` | string | Path to a file containing the secret value |
| `hotSwap` | boolean | Request hotswap deployment when supported |
| `confirm` | boolean | Required for mutating/destructive commands in `run` |
| `timeoutMs` | number | Command timeout in milliseconds for `run` |

The `list` action also accepts `category` (e.g. `deployment`, `diagnostics`, `secrets`) and `safety` (e.g. `readOnly`, `mutating`, `destructive`) to filter results.


> **Warning:** Always use `action: "plan"` before `action: "run"`. Commands marked as `sensitiveOutput` in CLI metadata have their output masked before being returned. The MCP planning guidance specifically warns assistants not to display `secret:get` or private `param:get` values in chat — run those in your own terminal to avoid exposing values in the conversation transcript.


### Dev tool

`stacktape_dev` manages [dev mode](/local-development/dev-mode-overview) lifecycle without requiring a terminal. The dev mode agent listens on a local HTTP port (default `7331`) for control commands.

- **`plan`** scans the project and returns suggested start arguments without starting anything. `plan` defaults `stage` to `dev` and infers `projectName`, `region`, `awsAccount`, `currentWorkingDirectory`, and `configPath` from deploy scripts and project scan when available. A dev-mode plan is only complete when `stage`, `region`, and `configPath` are resolved; otherwise the tool returns `MISSING_ARGS` with next steps. Since `stage` defaults to `dev`, the most common missing arguments are `region` and `configPath`.
- **`start`** launches dev mode as a subprocess with a 12-minute timeout for initial setup. Pass `projectName`, `stage`, `region`, `configPath`, and optionally `awsAccount`, `agentPort`, `currentWorkingDirectory`, `devMode`, `resources`, `skipResources`, `watch`, `remoteResources`, or `freshDb`.
- **`status`** returns the current state of the running session.
- **`logs`** paginates structured log entries. Accepts `cursor`, `limit`, and `workload` for filtering.
- **`rebuild`** triggers a rebuild of a single workload (pass `workload` name). **`rebuild_all`** triggers a rebuild of every workload.
- **`stop`** terminates the active session.

Only one dev session can be active at a time within an MCP server process. Actions other than `plan` and `start` require an active session — if none exists or the session's port no longer responds to health checks, the tool returns `NOT_FOUND`.

## Safety model

The MCP server classifies every CLI command into a safety level and enforces per-level gates:

| Safety level | Behavior | Examples |
|-------------|----------|---------|
| `readOnly` | Executes without confirmation | `diff`, `synth`, `info:stack`, `info:stacks`, `logs`, `metrics`, `alarms` |
| `diagnostic` | Executes without confirmation | `query:sql`, `query:dynamodb`, `query:redis`, `query:opensearch` |
| `local` | Executes without confirmation | `package` |
| `mutating` | Requires `confirm: true` in the `run` call | [`deploy`](/cli/deploy), [`secret:set`](/cli/secret-set), [`rollback`](/cli/rollback) |
| `destructive` | Requires direct user confirmation through MCP form elicitation; agent-supplied `confirm: true` alone is not sufficient | [`delete`](/cli/delete), [`secret:delete`](/cli/secret-delete) |
| `interactive` | Rejected by the MCP server — must be run in the user's own terminal | [`login`](/cli/login), [`dev`](/cli/dev) (use `stacktape_dev` instead), `bastion:session` |

For destructive commands, the MCP server uses the MCP elicitation protocol to prompt the user directly through their AI client's UI. If the client does not support form elicitation, the command fails closed and the assistant is told to ask the user to run the command in their own terminal.

Commands marked as `sensitiveOutput` in CLI metadata have their output masked before being returned. The MCP planning guidance specifically warns assistants not to display `secret:get` or private `param:get` values in chat. The MCP server also redacts known credential patterns (Stacktape API keys, GitHub tokens, Slack tokens, database connection strings with passwords) from all tool responses.

## Authentication

The MCP server reuses the local Stacktape CLI authentication state. It is not a separate identity provider, and planning does not require Stacktape credentials. Commands that run the underlying CLI may also require whatever AWS access that CLI command normally requires. The `stacktape_cli` planning action rejects raw Stacktape credential CLI arguments (such as `apiKey` or `STACKTAPE_API_KEY`), and the MCP instructions tell assistants not to collect API keys in chat.

- **Docs search, project scanning, CLI listing, description, and planning** work without any authentication.
- **`stacktape_cli` with `action: "run"` and `stacktape_dev`** execute the underlying Stacktape CLI flow and may require the same project, stage, region, AWS account, and profile setup as the CLI command. If execution fails with an authentication error, the MCP guidance tells the assistant to ask you to run [`stacktape login`](/cli/login) in your own terminal.

For CI environments, the MCP server's safety guidance tells assistants to ask users to configure a dedicated `STACKTAPE_API_KEY` secret outside the conversation instead of pasting credentials into chat.

## How it works

The MCP server starts when your AI client launches the `stacktape mcp` command as a child process using stdio transport. The startup sequence is:

1. The client spawns `stacktape mcp` as a child process.
2. The server builds a search index from generated LLM documentation files bundled with the CLI.
3. Four tools (docs, project, CLI, dev) are registered with the MCP protocol.
4. The assistant sends JSON-RPC tool calls over stdin; the server executes them and returns structured JSON over stdout.
